docs.title: FlowPilot
docs.text:
Authorization-as-a-Service for Agentic Workflows

FlowPilot is a managed authorization platform that handles complex access control for applications with AI agents, delegated authority, and multi-persona workflows.

docs.title: What is FlowPilot?
docs.text:
FlowPilot provides Authorization-as-a-Service for applications that need:

  • 🤖 AI Agent Authorization - Control what agents can do on behalf of users
  • 👥 Multi-Persona Support - Same user, different roles (traveler, agent, admin)
  • 🔗 Delegation Management - Users delegate authority to agents or other users
  • 📋 Policy-Driven Decisions - Declarative policies with consent and risk evaluation
  • 🔒 Privacy-First Design - No PII exposure, minimal token claims

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Your app authenticates users with Firebase (or bring your own identity provider)
  2. Your app makes REST API calls to FlowPilot for authorization decisions
  3. FlowPilot evaluates policies, delegation chains, and user attributes
  4. Your app enforces the decision (allow/deny with reason codes)

docs.title: The Travel Booking Metaphor
docs.text:
The concrete domain used is travel booking, but this is deliberately a metaphor for a generic "workflow execution" problem involving users, agents, and delegated authority.

  • A trip is a workflow
  • Booking steps are workflow items
  • Travelers can delegate actions to travel agents and AI agents
  • Auto-execution preferences drive policy-driven constraints

This maps cleanly to other domains:

  • Financial approvals
  • Medical record handling
  • Case management
  • Enterprise automation
  • Agent-based task execution

docs.title: For Developers Building
docs.text:
• AI-powered applications with autonomous agents
  • Workflow automation systems with delegated execution
  • Multi-tenant SaaS with complex permission models
  • Enterprise apps requiring auditability and compliance
  • Healthcare/Finance apps with strict access control requirements

docs.title: Why Not Just Use OAuth/OIDC?
docs.text:
OAuth/OIDC handles authentication (who are you?), but FlowPilot handles authorization (what can you do?):

  • OAuth: "This is Alice"
  • FlowPilot: "Alice (as a traveler) can delegate to Agent-X, who can auto-book if risk < 50 and consent = true"

FlowPilot integrates with your auth provider (Firebase, Auth0, Keycloak, etc.), not instead of it.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Integrate with Your App

    Add FlowPilot authorization to your web or mobile app

    Integration Guide

  • API Reference

    Explore the REST APIs with interactive documentation

    API Docs

  • Understanding Personas & Delegation

    Learn how multi-persona auth and delegation work

    Concepts

  • Writing Policies

    Create custom authorization policies for your use case

    Policy Guide

docs.title: 🔐 Defense-in-Depth Security
docs.text:
• JWKS-based JWT validation (zero network calls per request)
  • 4-layer input validation (Pydantic, path params, string sanitization, request size limits)
  • Injection attack prevention
  • Security headers on all responses
  • Production-safe error handling
  • Zero PII exposure to LLMs

docs.title: 🔗 Relationship-Based Access Control
docs.text:
• Explicit delegation graph management
  • Transitive delegation chain resolution
  • Workflow-scoped and global delegations
  • Time-bound and revocable delegations

docs.title: 📋 Policy-Driven Authorization
docs.text:
• Declarative Rego policies in OPA
  • Attribute-based access control (ABAC)
  • Consent and risk threshold evaluation
  • Explainable authorization decisions

docs.title: 🚀 Production-Ready Patterns
docs.text:
• Microservices architecture
  • Docker Compose for local development
  • Cloud Run deployment for GCP
  • Comprehensive testing suite
  • OpenAPI specifications for all APIs

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text:
• Backend: Python + FastAPI
  • Policy Engine: Open Policy Agent (OPA)
  • Authentication: Keycloak (local) / Firebase (GCP)
  • Database: SQLite (local) / PostgreSQL (GCP)
  • Infrastructure: Docker Compose / Google Cloud Run
  • Client: Swift (macOS desktop app)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
For App Developers: Start with the Integration Guide to add FlowPilot to your application.

For Platform Operators: See the Self-Hosting Guide if you want to run your own instance.

To Understand the Model: Read the Architecture Overview and Personas Guide.

docs.title: CORS Configuration
docs.text: FlowPilot services support Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) to enable web applications running in browsers to access the APIs.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
CORS is required when your web application's frontend (e.g., running on https://app.example.com) needs to make requests to FlowPilot APIs (running on different domains like https://flowpilot-domain-services-api-*.run.app).

All five FlowPilot services include CORS middleware: - authz-api (port 8002 local, GCP Cloud Run) - domain-services-api (port 8003 local, GCP Cloud Run) - delegation-api (port 8005 local, GCP Cloud Run) - user-profile-api (port 8006 local, GCP Cloud Run) - ai-agent-api (port 8004 local, GCP Cloud Run)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: CORS behavior is controlled via environment variables in the shared security.py library:

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Default Example CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS Comma-separated list of allowed origins, or * for all * https://app.example.com,https://admin.example.com CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS Allow credentials (cookies, auth headers) true true or false CORS_ALLOW_METHODS Allowed HTTP methods, or * for all * GET,POST,PUT,DELETE CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS Allowed headers, or * for all * Authorization,Content-Type

docs.title: Local Development (Docker Compose)
docs.text:
All services are configured with permissive CORS settings in docker-compose.yml:

environment:
  - CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=*
  - CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS=true
  - CORS_ALLOW_METHODS=*
  - CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS=*

This allows any web application running on localhost (e.g., http://localhost:3000, http://localhost:5173) to access the APIs during development.

docs.title: Production (GCP Cloud Run)
docs.text:
CORS settings for production are defined in cloud-run-envs/*.yaml files:

  • cloud-run-envs/authz-api.yaml
  • cloud-run-envs/domain-services-api.yaml
  • cloud-run-envs/delegation-api.yaml
  • cloud-run-envs/user-profile-api.yaml
  • cloud-run-envs/ai-agent-api.yaml

docs.title: Default Production Settings
docs.text:
Currently configured with permissive settings for development/testing:

CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS: "*"
CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS: "true"
CORS_ALLOW_METHODS: "*"
CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS: "*"

docs.title: Recommended Production Settings
docs.text:
For production deployments, restrict origins to your actual web application domains:

# Example: Production CORS configuration
CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS: "https://app.example.com,https://admin.example.com"
CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS: "true"
CORS_ALLOW_METHODS: "GET,POST,PUT,DELETE"
CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS: "Authorization,Content-Type,X-Request-ID"

docs.title: Development vs. Production
docs.text:
Development: - Using CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=* is acceptable for local development - Simplifies testing with various frontend frameworks and ports

Production: - Never use * in production - this allows any website to access your APIs - Specify exact origins (protocol + domain + port if non-standard) - Use HTTPS origins only (except localhost for development)

docs.title: Credentials and Wildcard Origins
docs.text:
Important: When CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS=true, you cannot use CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=* according to the CORS specification. Browsers will reject such requests.

If you need credentials (JWT tokens in Authorization header), you must specify exact origins:

# ✅ Correct: specific origins with credentials
CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS: "https://app.example.com"
CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS: "true"

# ❌ Incorrect: wildcard with credentials (browsers reject)
CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS: "*"
CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS: "true"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Restrict Origins: Only allow your actual frontend application domains
  2. Use HTTPS: Production origins should use HTTPS (except localhost for dev)
  3. Limit Methods: Only allow HTTP methods your API actually uses
  4. Limit Headers: Only allow headers your API requires
  5. Monitor: Log CORS errors to detect misconfiguration or unauthorized access attempts

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text:
1. Edit docker-compose.yml for the relevant service(s)
  2. Rebuild and restart: 
    docker compose up -d --build flowpilot-domain-services-api

docs.title: GCP Cloud Run
docs.text:
1. Edit the relevant cloud-run-envs/*.yaml file
  2. Rebuild the service (triggers image rebuild with new shared library): 
    gcloud builds submit --config=cloudbuild-domain-services-api.yaml
  3. Redeploy with updated environment variables: 
    gcloud run deploy flowpilot-domain-services-api \
  --image=us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/vision-course-476214/flowpilot/flowpilot-domain-services-api:latest \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --platform=managed \
  --env-vars-file=cloud-run-envs/domain-services-api.yaml

docs.title: Browser DevTools
docs.text:
1. Open your web application in a browser
  2. Open DevTools → Network tab
  3. Make a request to a FlowPilot API
  4. Check the response headers for: 
    access-control-allow-origin: https://your-app.example.com
access-control-allow-credentials: true
access-control-allow-methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
access-control-allow-headers: authorization, content-type

docs.title: CORS Preflight Requests
docs.text:
For requests with custom headers (like Authorization), browsers send a preflight OPTIONS request:

# Example preflight request
curl -X OPTIONS http://localhost:8003/v1/workflows \
  -H "Origin: http://localhost:3000" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type" \
  -v

You should see CORS headers in the response.

docs.title: Common CORS Errors
docs.text:
Error: "Access to fetch at '...' from origin '...' has been blocked by CORS policy"

Causes: 1. Origin not in CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS 2. Using * with CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS=true 3. Missing required header in CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS 4. Service not configured with CORS middleware (should not happen in FlowPilot)

Solution: - Check environment variables in running service - Verify origin matches exactly (including protocol and port) - Check browser DevTools → Console for specific CORS error details

docs.title: Implementation Details
docs.text:
CORS is implemented in flowpilot-services/shared-libraries/security.py:

def get_cors_config() -> dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Get CORS configuration from environment variables.
    Returns dict suitable for passing to CORSMiddleware constructor.
    """
    origins_str = os.environ.get("CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS", "*")
    origins = [o.strip() for o in origins_str.split(",")] if origins_str != "*" else ["*"]

    allow_credentials = os.environ.get("CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS", "true").lower() == "true"

    methods_str = os.environ.get("CORS_ALLOW_METHODS", "*")
    methods = [m.strip() for m in methods_str.split(",")] if methods_str != "*" else ["*"]

    headers_str = os.environ.get("CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS", "*")
    headers = [h.strip() for h in headers_str.split(",")] if headers_str != "*" else ["*"]

    return {
        "allow_origins": origins,
        "allow_credentials": allow_credentials,
        "allow_methods": methods,
        "allow_headers": headers,
    }

Each service applies CORS middleware in its FastAPI app initialization:

from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
import security

# Add CORS middleware
cors_config = security.get_cors_config()
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=cors_config["allow_origins"],
    allow_credentials=cors_config["allow_credentials"],
    allow_methods=cors_config["allow_methods"],
    allow_headers=cors_config["allow_headers"],
)

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Integration Guide - Web application integration examples
  • Security Architecture - Overall security model
  • Deployment Guide - GCP Cloud Run deployment with CORS

docs.title: AI Agent API
docs.text: The AI Agent API executes workflows on behalf of users with per-item authorization.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Base URL (Local): http://localhost:8004 Base URL (GCP): https://flowpilot-ai-agent-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app

docs.title: AuthZ API
docs.text: The AuthZ API serves as the authorization façade for FlowPilot, implementing the Policy Decision Point (PDP) pattern with AuthZEN-compliant request structure.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Base URL (Local): http://localhost:8002 Base URL (GCP): https://flowpilot-authz-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Policy evaluation using OPA Rego policies (PDP)
  • Authorization graph validation via delegation-api
  • JWT validation and claim extraction
  • Structured authorization decisions with reason codes

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• ✅ AuthZEN-compliant request/response structure
  • ✅ Stateless operation (no in-memory storage)
  • ✅ Bearer token authentication on all endpoints (except /health)
  • ✅ Integration with OPA for ABAC decisions
  • ✅ Integration with delegation-api for ReBAC checks

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All endpoints (except /health) require a Bearer token in the Authorization header:

Local (Keycloak)GCP (Firebase) 
Authorization: Bearer <keycloak-jwt-token>

Authorization: Bearer <firebase-id-token>

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
GET /health - No authentication required

Returns service health status.

docs.title: Evaluate Authorization
docs.text:
POST /v1/evaluate - Requires authentication

Evaluates whether a subject (agent or user) may perform an action on a resource.

Request Structure: 

{
  "subject": {
    "type": "agent",
    "id": "agent_flowpilot_1"
  },
  "action": {
    "name": "execute"
  },
  "resource": {
    "type": "workflow_item",
    "id": "i_f032d0a2",
    "properties": {
      "workflow_id": "t_4a6455a3"
    }
  },
  "context": {
    "principal": {
      "id": "1460e175-74f9-43af-aac3-7b4fc0547f05"
    }
  },
  "options": {
    "dry_run": true,
    "explain": true
  }
}

Response Structure: 

{
  "decision": "allow",
  "reason_codes": [],
  "advice": []
}

docs.title: Decision Flow
docs.text:
1. JWT Validation: Validate bearer token and extract claims
  2. Delegation Check: If subject ≠ principal, query delegation-api (ReBAC)
  3. Policy Evaluation: Build OPA input and query OPA (ABAC)
  4. Return Decision: Structured response with decision, reason codes, and advice

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Status Code Description 200 Authorization decision returned 400 Bad request or downstream failure 401 Unauthorized (invalid token) 403 Forbidden 422 Validation error 500 Internal server error

docs.title: cURL Example
docs.text:
# Get token
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  "http://localhost:8080/realms/flowpilot/protocol/openid-connect/token" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=flowpilot-agent" \
  -d "client_secret=$AGENT_CLIENT_SECRET" | jq -r '.access_token')

# Make authorization request
curl -X POST http://localhost:8002/v1/evaluate \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "subject": {"type": "agent", "id": "agent-runner"},
    "action": {"name": "execute"},
    "resource": {
      "type": "workflow_item",
      "id": "i_abc123",
      "properties": {"workflow_id": "w_xyz789"}
    },
    "context": {
      "principal": {"id": "user-uuid"}
    }
  }'

docs.title: Python Example
docs.text:
import requests

headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json"
}

authz_request = {
    "subject": {"type": "agent", "id": "agent-runner"},
    "action": {"name": "execute"},
    "resource": {
        "type": "workflow_item",
        "id": "i_abc123",
        "properties": {"workflow_id": "w_xyz789"}
    },
    "context": {
        "principal": {"id": "user-uuid"}
    }
}

response = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:8002/v1/evaluate",
    json=authz_request,
    headers=headers
)

decision = response.json()
print(f"Decision: {decision['decision']}")

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Authorization Architecture
  • OPA Policy Development
  • Delegation API

docs.title: Delegation API
docs.text: The Delegation API manages delegation relationships (ReBAC) for FlowPilot.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Base URL (Local): http://localhost:8005 Base URL (GCP): https://flowpilot-delegation-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app

docs.title: Domain Services API
docs.text: The Domain Services API is the workflow system-of-record, managing trips (workflows) and booking items (workflow items).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Base URL (Local): http://localhost:8003 Base URL (GCP): https://flowpilot-domain-services-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app

docs.title: User Profile API
docs.text: The User Profile API provides an abstraction layer over Firebase/Firestore for user profile management.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Base URL (Local): http://localhost:8006 Base URL (GCP): https://flowpilot-user-profile-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Get user profile (authenticated user's own profile)
  • Update user profile (personas and autobook preferences)
  • List users by persona (for delegation candidate discovery)

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• ✅ Clean abstraction over Firebase (no Firebase SDK required in clients)
  • ✅ JWT authentication on all endpoints (except /health)
  • ✅ Users can only access/modify their own profile
  • ✅ Input validation and sanitization
  • ✅ Zero PII exposure (only UUID and persona processed)

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All endpoints (except /health) require a Bearer token:

Local (Keycloak)GCP (Firebase) 
Authorization: Bearer <keycloak-jwt-token>

Authorization: Bearer <firebase-id-token>

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text: GET /health - No authentication required

docs.title: Get User Profile
docs.text:
GET /v1/profile - Returns authenticated user's profile

Response: 

{
  "sub": "df3438a6-7523-4a59-8339-41e073be3148",
  "personas": ["traveler"],
  "autobook_consent": true,
  "autobook_price": 500,
  "autobook_leadtime": 30,
  "autobook_risklevel": 50
}

docs.title: Update User Profile
docs.text:
PUT /v1/profile - Update profile (partial update)

Request Body: 

{
  "personas": ["traveler", "travel-agent"],
  "autobook_consent": true,
  "autobook_price": 1000,
  "autobook_leadtime": 60,
  "autobook_risklevel": 75
}

All fields are optional. Only provided fields will be updated.

Response: Updated profile (same format as GET)

docs.title: List Users by Persona
docs.text:
GET /v1/users?persona={persona} - Find delegation candidates

Query Parameters: - persona (required): Persona to filter by (e.g., "travel-agent")

Response: 

{
  "users": [
    {
      "sub": "bcadc299-f463-4f7d-bab5-2221761387f4",
      "email": "agent@example.com",
      "persona": "travel-agent"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Get Own Profile
docs.text:
TOKEN="your-firebase-or-keycloak-token"

curl -X GET http://localhost:8006/v1/profile \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"

docs.title: Update Personas
docs.text:
curl -X PUT http://localhost:8006/v1/profile \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "personas": ["traveler", "travel-agent"]
  }'

docs.title: Update Autobook Preferences
docs.text:
curl -X PUT http://localhost:8006/v1/profile \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "autobook_consent": true,
    "autobook_price": 1000,
    "autobook_leadtime": 60,
    "autobook_risklevel": 75
  }'

docs.title: Find Travel Agents
docs.text:
curl -X GET "http://localhost:8006/v1/users?persona=travel-agent" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"

docs.title: Persona Values
docs.text:
Allowed persona values are configurable. Default values:

  • traveler - End user booking travel
  • travel-agent - Professional travel agent
  • admin - System administrator

docs.title: Validation Rules
docs.text:
• personas: Non-empty list, max 5 personas per user
  • autobook_price: Non-negative integer
  • autobook_leadtime: Non-negative integer (days)
  • autobook_risklevel: Integer between 0-100

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Status Code Description 200 Success 400 Validation error (invalid persona, out of range values) 401 Unauthorized (invalid or missing token) 404 Profile not found 500 Internal server error

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:

  • Understanding Personas - Learn about multi-persona authorization
  • Delegation API - Manage delegation relationships

docs.title: Self-Hosting Overview
docs.text: FlowPilot is offered as a managed service, but you can also self-host your own instance.

docs.title: Why Self-Host?
docs.text:
Consider self-hosting if you:

  • Need full control over infrastructure
  • Have strict data residency requirements
  • Want to customize policies beyond what the managed service offers
  • Are building a white-label solution

docs.title: Deployment Options
docs.text:
• Local Development: Docker Compose for testing and development
  • GCP Cloud Run: Production-ready deployment on Google Cloud
  • Custom: Deploy to any Kubernetes cluster or cloud provider

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Self-hosting requires:

  • Infrastructure expertise (Docker, cloud platforms)
  • Security expertise (JWT validation, TLS, secrets management)
  • Policy authoring expertise (Rego/OPA)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
For most developers, we recommend using the managed service at the production endpoints listed in the Integration Guide.

If you still want to self-host, see: - Local Development - Docker Compose setup - GCP Cloud Run - Production deployment

docs.title: Authentication Setup
docs.text: Learn how to set up Firebase Authentication for your FlowPilot integration.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FlowPilot uses Firebase Authentication tokens to identify users. You'll need to:

  1. Create a Firebase project
  2. Enable authentication methods
  3. Configure your app with Firebase
  4. Get ID tokens to pass to FlowPilot APIs

docs.title: Coming Soon
docs.text:
Detailed setup instructions for Firebase Authentication across web, iOS, and Android platforms.

For now, see the Integration Guide for quick examples.

docs.title: Your First Workflow
docs.text: A step-by-step tutorial to create and execute your first workflow with FlowPilot.

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text:
In this tutorial, you'll:

  1. Authenticate a user
  2. Create a simple workflow
  3. Set up delegation to an AI agent
  4. Execute the workflow
  5. Handle the results

Complete tutorial with code samples for web and mobile.

For now, see the Integration Guide for a complete working example.

docs.title: Gcp Deployment

docs.title: Integration Guide
docs.text: Add FlowPilot authorization to your web or mobile application.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FlowPilot is a managed authorization service that you call via REST APIs. It handles:

  • Multi-persona authorization (user as traveler vs. agent vs. admin)
  • Delegation management (user delegates to AI agent or another user)
  • Policy evaluation (consent checks, risk thresholds, custom rules)
  • Audit trails and explainable decisions

docs.title: Service Endpoints
docs.text:
Production API Base URL: https://flowpilot-domain-services-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app

All APIs require Firebase Authentication tokens.

docs.title: Available APIs
docs.text: API Purpose Endpoint Domain Services Manage workflows and workflow items /v1/workflows AuthZ Authorization decisions /v1/evaluate Delegation Manage delegations /v1/delegations User Profile User personas and preferences /v1/profile AI Agent Execute workflows via agent /v1/workflow-runs

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Firebase Project - Sign up at firebase.google.com
  2. FlowPilot Account - Contact us to get your project configured
  3. User Profile Storage - Set up Firestore to store user personas and preferences

docs.title: Step 1: Authenticate Your Users
docs.text:
FlowPilot uses Firebase Authentication for user identity. Integrate Firebase Auth in your app:

Web (JavaScript)iOS (Swift)Android (Kotlin) 
import { getAuth, signInWithEmailAndPassword } from 'firebase/auth';

const auth = getAuth();
const userCredential = await signInWithEmailAndPassword(
  auth, 
  email, 
  password
);

// Get ID token to pass to FlowPilot
const idToken = await userCredential.user.getIdToken();

import FirebaseAuth

Auth.auth().signIn(withEmail: email, password: password) { result, error in
    guard let user = result?.user else { return }

    user.getIDToken { token, error in
        // Use token with FlowPilot APIs
        let idToken = token
    }
}

import com.google.firebase.auth.FirebaseAuth

val auth = FirebaseAuth.getInstance()
auth.signInWithEmailAndPassword(email, password)
    .addOnCompleteListener { task ->
        if (task.isSuccessful) {
            val user = auth.currentUser
            user?.getIdToken(true)?.addOnCompleteListener { tokenTask ->
                val idToken = tokenTask.result?.token
                // Use token with FlowPilot APIs
            }
        }
    }

docs.title: Step 2: Manage User Profiles
docs.text: FlowPilot provides a User Profile API to manage user personas and preferences. No Firebase SDK required.

docs.title: Get User Profile
docs.text:
const response = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-user-profile-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/profile',
  {
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`
    }
  }
);

const profile = await response.json();
console.log('User persona:', profile.personas);

docs.title: Update User Profile
docs.text:
const response = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-user-profile-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/profile',
  {
    method: 'PUT',
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`,
      'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      personas: ["traveler"],
      autobook_consent: true,
      autobook_price: 500,
      autobook_leadtime: 30,
      autobook_risklevel: 50
    })
  }
);

const updatedProfile = await response.json();

docs.title: Find Users by Persona
docs.text:
Find delegation candidates (e.g., travel agents):

const response = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-user-profile-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/users?persona=travel-agent',
  {
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`
    }
  }
);

const { users } = await response.json();
console.log('Available agents:', users);

docs.title: Profile Fields
docs.text: Field Type Description sub string User UUID (from Firebase Auth) personas string[] List of personas: ["traveler", "travel-agent", "admin"] autobook_consent boolean Consent for AI agent execution autobook_price number Max price agent can book autobook_leadtime number Max days in advance autobook_risklevel number Risk tolerance (0-100)

docs.title: Step 3: Create Workflows
docs.text:
Call the Domain Services API to create a workflow (e.g., a travel booking):

const response = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-domain-services-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/workflows',
  {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`,
      'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      template_id: 'trip-to-milan',
      principal_sub: user.uid,
      start_date: '2026-02-01',
      persona: 'traveler'
    })
  }
);

const workflow = await response.json();
console.log('Created workflow:', workflow.workflow_id);

docs.title: Step 4: Delegate to AI Agent
docs.text:
Allow the AI agent to execute workflows on the user's behalf:

const delegationResponse = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-delegation-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/delegations',
  {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`,
      'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      principal_id: user.uid,
      delegate_id: 'agent-runner',  // FlowPilot's AI agent ID
      workflow_id: workflow.workflow_id,
      scope: ['execute'],
      expires_in_days: 7
    })
  }
);

docs.title: Step 5: Execute Workflow via Agent
docs.text:
Trigger the AI agent to execute the workflow:

const runResponse = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-ai-agent-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/workflow-runs',
  {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`,
      'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      workflow_id: workflow.workflow_id,
      principal_sub: user.uid,
      dry_run: false,
      persona: 'traveler'
    })
  }
);

const runResult = await runResponse.json();

// Check results
runResult.results.forEach(item => {
  console.log(`Item ${item.workflow_item_id}: ${item.decision}`);
  if (item.decision === 'deny') {
    console.log('Reason:', item.reason_codes);
  }
});

docs.title: Step 6: Handle Authorization Decisions
docs.text:
The agent returns structured authorization decisions:

{
  "decision": "deny",
  "reason_codes": ["rego.deny.consent_required"],
  "advice": [
    {
      "type": "deny",
      "message": "User consent not provided for auto-booking"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Common Reason Codes
docs.text: Reason Code Meaning rego.deny.consent_required User hasn't consented to auto-booking rego.deny.price_exceeded Item price exceeds user's threshold rego.deny.leadtime_exceeded Booking too far in advance rego.deny.risk_exceeded Risk level too high for user delegation.not_found No delegation exists delegation.expired Delegation has expired

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
Here's a complete workflow in JavaScript:

import { initializeApp } from 'firebase/app';
import { getAuth, signInWithEmailAndPassword } from 'firebase/auth';

// 1. Initialize Firebase
const app = initializeApp(firebaseConfig);
const auth = getAuth(app);

// 2. Sign in user
const userCredential = await signInWithEmailAndPassword(auth, email, password);
const idToken = await userCredential.user.getIdToken();

// 3. Create workflow
const workflowResponse = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-domain-services-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/workflows',
  {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`,
      'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      template_id: 'trip-to-milan',
      principal_sub: userCredential.user.uid,
      start_date: '2026-02-01',
      persona: 'traveler'
    })
  }
);
const workflow = await workflowResponse.json();

// 4. Delegate to agent
await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-delegation-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/delegations',
  {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`,
      'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      principal_id: userCredential.user.uid,
      delegate_id: 'agent-runner',
      workflow_id: workflow.workflow_id,
      expires_in_days: 7
    })
  }
);

// 5. Execute via agent
const runResponse = await fetch(
  'https://flowpilot-ai-agent-api-737191827545.us-central1.run.app/v1/workflow-runs',
  {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${idToken}`,
      'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      workflow_id: workflow.workflow_id,
      principal_sub: userCredential.user.uid,
      dry_run: false
    })
  }
);
const result = await runResponse.json();

console.log('Execution results:', result);

docs.title: SDK Support
docs.text:
Currently, FlowPilot provides REST APIs. SDKs are planned for:

  • JavaScript/TypeScript (coming soon)
  • Swift/iOS (coming soon)
  • Kotlin/Android (coming soon)
  • Python (coming soon)

For now, use standard HTTP clients in your language of choice.

docs.title: Rate Limits
docs.text:
• Development: 100 requests/minute
  • Production: 1000 requests/minute
  • Enterprise: Custom limits

Contact us for higher limits.

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
• Documentation: API Reference
  • Email: support@flowpilot.dev
  • Discord: Join our community

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Understand Personas - Learn about multi-persona authorization
  • Working with Delegations - Deep dive into delegation management
  • Writing Custom Policies - Create policies for your use case
  • API Reference - Explore all available APIs

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text: Documentation coming soon.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get FlowPilot running locally in 5 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker and Docker Compose
  • make (optional, for convenience commands)
  • curl or similar HTTP client for testing

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/flowpilot.git
cd flowpilot

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Environment
docs.text:
Create a .env file in the root directory:

cat > .env << EOF
KEYCLOAK_ADMIN_USERNAME=admin
KEYCLOAK_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin123
KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET=your-secret-here
AGENT_CLIENT_SECRET=your-agent-secret-here
EOF

Security Note

Never commit the .env file to version control. It contains sensitive credentials.

docs.title: Step 3: Start the Stack
docs.text:
The easiest way to start FlowPilot is using the provided script:

make up
# Or directly:
./bin/stack-up.sh

This script will:

  1. Build and start all Docker containers
  2. Wait for Keycloak to be ready (can take 60+ seconds)
  3. Provision test users and agent relationships
  4. Configure OIDC clients

docs.title: Step 4: Verify Services Are Running
docs.text:
Check the status of all services:

make status
# Or:
docker compose ps

You should see all services in "Up" status:

  • keycloak
  • opa
  • flowpilot-authz-api
  • flowpilot-delegation-api
  • flowpilot-domain-services-api
  • flowpilot-ai-agent-api

docs.title: Step 5: Run Integration Tests
docs.text:
Verify everything works by running the test suite:

python3 tests/user_based_testing.py

Expected output: 

Test Results:
- Kathleen: Allowed=0, Denied=3, Errors=0 ✓
- Peter: Allowed=0, Denied=3, Errors=0 ✓
- Carlo: Allowed=3, Denied=0, Errors=0 ✓

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8002/health  # authz-api
curl http://localhost:8003/health  # domain-services-api
curl http://localhost:8004/health  # ai-agent-api
curl http://localhost:8005/health  # delegation-api

docs.title: Get an Access Token
docs.text:
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  "http://localhost:8080/realms/flowpilot/protocol/openid-connect/token" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=flowpilot-agent" \
  -d "client_secret=$AGENT_CLIENT_SECRET" | jq -r '.access_token')

echo $TOKEN

docs.title: Create a Workflow
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8003/v1/workflows \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "template_id": "trip-to-milan",
    "principal_sub": "user-uuid-here",
    "start_date": "2026-02-01",
    "persona": "traveler"
  }'

docs.title: View Logs
docs.text:
# All services
make logs

# Specific service
docker compose logs -f flowpilot-authz-api
docker compose logs -f opa

docs.title: Stop the Stack
docs.text:
make down
# Or:
./bin/stack-down.sh

docs.title: Reset Everything
docs.text:
make reset
# Or:
./bin/stack-reset.sh

This wipes all volumes and gives you a clean slate.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• 📖 Local Development Guide - Detailed setup and development workflow
  • 🏗️ Architecture Overview - Understand the design
  • 🔐 Authorization Model - Learn about personas and delegation
  • 🚀 Deploy to GCP - Run in the cloud

docs.title: Keycloak Not Ready
docs.text:
If provisioning fails, wait longer:

docker compose logs -f keycloak
# Wait for "Listening on: https://0.0.0.0:8443"

docs.title: Port Conflicts
docs.text:
Ensure ports 8080, 8181, 8443, and 8002-8005 are available:

lsof -i :8080
lsof -i :8002

docs.title: Services Won't Start
docs.text:
Try a full rebuild:

docker compose down -v
docker compose up -d --build

For more troubleshooting tips, see the Troubleshooting Guide.
